coredata.dc:title: Is the interaction between graphene oxide and minerals reversible?
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xia
Sun, Ju
Xu, Xuetao
Sheng, Guodong
Sun, Yubing
Huang, Yongshun
Alsaedi, Ahmed
Hayat, Tasawar
Li, Jiaxing
coredata.dc:description: The increased applications and production of graphene oxide (GO) make the necessity to study information on the interaction of GO with minerals. In this work, adsorption and desorption were used to study the reversibility of interaction between GO and goethite/kaolinite. Result showed that the pH value, ionic strength, and temperature had significant effects on the adsorption and desorption behavior of GO. Interaction force was stronger between GO and goethite than that of kaolinite. The interaction may be attributed to the electrostatic, hydrogen-bonding, and Lewis acid base interactions. The irreversible interaction between GO and minerals may be a main mechanism for the observed desorption hysteresis. These results are important for evaluating the fate and health risk of GO in the environment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Interaction
Kaolinite
Goethite
Desorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite
Desorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite
The mechanism for interaction between GO and minerals

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra (A) and zeta potentials (B) for kaolinite, goethite and GO.

Kinetic models for goethite and kaolinite at pH 5.0. Pseudo-first-order (A) pseudo-second-order (B) intra-particle diffusion model (C) Adsorption (%) for goethite and kaolinite at different pH. m/V (goethite) = 6.0 g/L, m/V (kaolinite) = 6.0 g/L, m/V (GO) = 32.7 mg/L.

Adsorption (%) of GO on goethite (A) and kaolinite (B) at various concentrations of NaCl at pH 5.0 and 7.0. Zeta potentials (C) and Dh (D) of GO, goethite and kaolinite at various concentrations of NaCl at pH 7.0. GO = 32.7 mg/L, goethite = 6.0 g/L, kaolinite = 6.0 g/L. Adsorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite at different temperature at pH 5.0 (E), Goethite = 6.0 g/L, kaolinite = 6.0 g/L, GO = 32.7 mg/L. Adsorption isotherms of GO on kaolinite and goethite at pH 5.0 and 7.0, goethite = 6.0 g/L, kaolinite = 6.0 g/L (F).

The amount of GO desorption from goethite and kaolinite under different time at pH 5.0 (A). Desorption kinetic model of GO desorption from goethite and kaolinite (B). Desorption (%) of GO from kaolinite and goethite at different pH. (C) Goethite = 6.0 g/L, kaolinite = 6.0 g/L, GO = 32.7 mg/L. Desorption (%) of GO from goethite (D) and kaolinite (E) at different NaCl concentration at pH 5.0 and 7.0. Desorption (%) of GO from kaolinite and goethite at different temperature at pH 5.0 (F). Goethite = 6.0 g/L, kaolinite = 6.0 g/L, GO = 32.7 mg/L.
SEM for GO-goethtie (A) and GO-kaolinite (B) FTIR for goethite (C) and kaolinite (D), GO, after adsorption and desorption of GO.

head.ce:title: Is the interaction between graphene oxide and minerals reversible?

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and Energy Conservation Materials, Institute of Plasma Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, P.O. Box 1126, Hefei, 230031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Photovoltaic and energy conservation materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Wuyi University, Jiangmen, 529020, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The increased applications and production of graphene oxide (GO) make the necessity to study information on the interaction of GO with minerals. In this work, adsorption and desorption were used to study the reversibility of interaction between GO and goethite/kaolinite. Result showed that the pH value, ionic strength, and temperature had significant effects on the adsorption and desorption behavior of GO. Interaction force was stronger between GO and goethite than that of kaolinite. The interaction may be attributed to the electrostatic, hydrogen-bonding, and Lewis acid base interactions. The irreversible interaction between GO and minerals may be a main mechanism for the observed desorption hysteresis. These results are important for evaluating the fate and health risk of GO in the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO), because of its unique physicochemical properties, shows tremendous potential for many applications (Chen et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2018a; Hu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2017a; Cheng et al., 2017). The broad production and extensive application of GO make it easy to release into natural aqueous systems during its production, transport, application, and disposal processes (Chen et al., 2018; Chowdhury et al., 2014; Hua et al., 2015). Moreover, the layered structure and the distribution of oxygen-containing functional groups on its basal planes and sheet edges lead to the dispersibility and high mobility in the aqueous environment (Chowdhury et al., 2013). The unique properties may lead to interaction between GO and various environmental components, which could potentially cause unexpected health or environmental hazards (Chng and Pumera, 2013; Liu et al., 2018b).

In natural environment, minerals are important components of sediments and soils, and present in aqueous environment (Keiluweit and Kleber, 2009). The high dispersibility of GO and the universal presence of minerals make them interact easily. Studying their adsorption-desorption behavior is one important tool to evaluate the interaction between GO and minerals, since this is the initial step in the interaction between GO and minerals (Xia et al., 2018). Many researchers have studied the interaction between GO and minerals by focusing on their adsorption behavior (Sotirelis and Chrysikopoulos, 2015; Zou et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2014). Sotirelis et al. investigated the adsorption of GO onto quartz sand under different pH, ionic strength and temperature (Sotirelis and Chrysikopoulos, 2015). Results showed that interaction between GO and quartz sand was governed by chemisorption. Layered double hydroxide as one of important clay minerals has been reported as secondary mineral in mine areas. Zou et al. found the interaction force between Mg/Al layered double hydroxide and GO was electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonds by adsorption experimental and theoretical calculation study (Zou et al., 2016). In the work of Ren et al., Al2O3 was applied to adsorb GO and results showed that interaction process depended on the solution pH and the types and concentrations of electrolytes (Ren et al., 2014). These studies investigated interaction between GO and minerals by their adsorption behavior. The result displayed that interaction was common and affected by many factors, e. g. solution pH, ionic strength and temperature.

Desorption behavior of contaminants provides more direct information to assess their risk in comparison to adsorption studies (Wang et al., 2013). Desorption hysteresis is very common in the adsorption of pollution by minerals (Yang and Xing, 2007; Kan et al., 1998; Fu et al., 1994). Banat et al. observed hysteresis in the adsorption/desorption of phenol by bentonite (Banat et al., 2000). Gunasekara and Xing found desorption hysteresis in adsorption and desorption of naphthalene by soil organic matter (Gunasekara and Xing, 2003). Zhao et al. have mentioned desorption hysteresis occurred on GO and goethite interaction process (Zhao et al., 2015). However, to date, there is no systematic study about the desorption behavior. Adsorption and desorption isotherms corresponding to the forward and backward reactions did not coincide, indicating that adsorption was not reversible (Pan et al., 2004). Therefore, studying desorption behavior can help understand the reversibility of GO/minerals interactions. Therefore, it is necessary to present a detailed study on the desorption behavior.

Kaolinite is an abundant clay material in natural environment, and carries negative charge on the surface (Wang et al., 2015). Goethite is one of the most common and stable crystalline iron (hydr)oxides in natural environments. It is also a mineral which is positive charged under neutral water environment (Antelo et al., 2005; Liu et al., 2017a). The difference between kaolinite and goethite in shape and charge distribution may result in their different interactions with GO (Sun et al., 2017b). Given their abundance and ubiquity, they were selected as representatives to investigate their interactions with GO.

The work presented is to investigate the reversibility of GO/minerals interactions. Kaolinite and goethite were selected as the representative minerals. Batch adsorption-desorption experiments were conducted at three controlled temperatures, at different pH values and ionic strengths. Chemical composition and morphologies before and after adsorption-desorption were characterization by FTIR (Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy) and SEM (scanning electron microscope). Study the mechanism of adsorption-desorption is important for assessing the risk of GO accurately.

Kaolinite was ordered from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and goethite was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The two kinds of clay and their FTIR data were the same as our previous work (Liu et al., 2017a). Other chemicals used were analytical grade and Milli-Q water was used throughout the experiment. GO was prepared by modified Hummers methods (Hummers and Offeman, 1958) and the procedures were described in detail in SI.

Adsorption experiment. The adsorption experiments were conducted by batch experiment in 10 mL polyethylene centrifuge tubes. 6.0 g/L goethite or kaolinite was added to centrifuge tubes filled with GO suspensions at different concentrations. The pH values were adjusted by adding negligible volume of 0.01–0.1M HCl or NaOH solution. The centrifuge tubes were placed on an oscillator and shaken at 150 rpm for 24 h to reach adsorption equilibrium. Then, the minerals were separated by centrifugation (3000 rpm, 10 min).

Adsorption isotherms were performed at GO concentrations within 13–82 mg/L and pH at 5.0 or 7.0 at 293, 308 and 318 K. For adsorption kinetics, 32.7 mg/L GO was used, pH was at 5.0 or 7.0 and the adsorption time ranged from 5 to 1440 min. The adsorption experiment for effect of pH was conducted at pH 2–10 with 32.7 mg/L GO and 6.0 g/L goethite or kaolinite. The adsorption percentage as a function of ionic strength was measured at 32.7 mg/L GO and 6.0 g/L goethite or kaolinite.

Desorption experiments were performed immediately after adsorption experiments. After removing 5 mL supernatants for GO determination, 5 mL deionized water which pH was the same as above supernatants was added. Then the tubes were shaken for 48 h. After equilibrium, the tubes were centrifuged and the GO concentration in the supernatants was detected. Specifically, for desorption isotherm experiments, 3 mL deionized water with the same pH value was amended after removing 3 mL supernatant. Then experiments were carried out under the same condition for adsorption isotherm experiments.

The adsorption percentage (%) and the amounts of adsorbed GO (q
e , mg g−1) were calculated from the difference between the initial concentration (C
0 ) and the equilibrium one (Ce), which can be expressed as Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively (Zhu et al., 2019; Tan et al., 2018a,b; Sun et al., 2019):
(1)
R % = (
C
0
-
C
e
) /
C
0
× 100 %
(2)
q
e
= (
C
0
-
C
e
) / m × V

The concentration of GO in desorption was calculated from:
(3)
q
e
' =
q
e
− (
C
e
'
-
C
e
/ 2 ) × V / m
where C
e ’ (mg/L) and Ce (mg/L) was the concentration of GO in the solution after desorption and the concentration of GO in the solution after adsorption, respectively. V (mL) is the volume of solution and m (g) is the mass of minerals in solution.

The zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) of minerals and GO at different pH and ionic strength were determined by Zetasizer Nanosizer ZS instrument (Malvern Instrument Co.). SEM was performed with a JEOL JSM-6330F system to characterize the morphologies. The FTIR spectra over a range from 400 to 4000 cm−1 were obtained on a Nicolet Magana-IR 750 spectrometer with pure KBr as the background. The UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu) was employed to measure concentration of GO by testing the absorbance at the wavelength of 227 nm. The atomic force microscope (AFM) images were acquired from Scanning Probe Microscope (SPA-300HV&SPI3800N, Seiko). The specific surface area was obtained using N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, which was conducted on a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 surface area analyzer at 77 K.

The morphology, chemical structure and surface charge of GO and minerals may influence their interaction process. SEM images of GO, kaolinite and goethite were given in Fig. S1. As shown in Fig. S1A, kaolinite had a schistose structure which was composed by Si–O tetrahedron and Al–O tetrahedron (Wang et al., 2011). The size of the flake was uneven and most of them were less than 1 μm. SEM of goethite (Fig. S1B) showed the agglomerates of needle-like particles and the length of the needles was also below 1 μm. Fig. S1C showed that the SEM of GO was a two-dimensional multilayered structure with lateral sizes of several nanometers, and individual GO sheets could be self-folded. The size of GO was not uniform, and the greatest length in the lateral dimension varied from 200 nm to 1 μm obtained from the AFM image (Fig. S1D).

As shown in FTIR spectra of kaolinite (Fig. 1 ), the broad peak around 3400 and 1632 cm−1 can be assigned to the -OH stretching and bending vibration of adsorbed water, respectively (Wang et al., 2011). The peak at 1105 cm−1 corresponded to the Si-O-Si stretching vibration and the peaks between 900 and 400 cm−1 were Si-O-Si bending vibration (Zhang et al., 2009). Goethite clearly showed the most characteristic bands from a structure at: 1632, 862 and 802 cm−1 corresponded to -OH bending. Moreover, the band from the Fe-O stretching at 640 cm−1 was further observed (Guo et al., 2013). The FTIR spectra of GO showed the characteristic peaks at 1500–1600, 1650–1750 cm−1, broad peak at 3050–3800 cm−1 were aromatic C-C, carboxyl and carbonyl, respectively (Zou et al., 2016; Yu et al., 2016).

The BET surface area is an important influencing factor for adsorption capacity, which gave values on the specific surface area of goethite and kaolinite (Ajouyed et al., 2010). Fig. S2 showed the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms for goethite and kaolinite and the specific surface area for goethite and kaolinite were 54.2 and 12.9 m2 g−1, respectively. The larger BET surface areas for goethite than kaolinte may promote their adsorption capacity for GO.

Zeta potential plays a crucial role in understanding the charge interactions mechanism because it is defined as the electrical potential developed at the solid–liquid interface in response to the relative movement of soil particles and water (Yang et al., 2016; Tan et al., 2018a,b; Tan et al., 2019). Fig. 1B presented the zeta potentials of GO and minerals as a function of pH. Result showed that as the pH of the solution increases, the zeta potentials of GO and minerals became more negative. Note that the zeta potential of goethite was higher than that of kaolinite and GO at pH < 9 whereas the difference of their zeta potential became smaller at pH > 9. The adsorption of H+ ions (used to adjust the pH of the solution) onto mineral surfaces compresses the diffuse electrical double layer and results in lower values for zeta potential. On the other hand, adsorption of OH− ions onto mineral surfaces results in a large diffuse electrical double layer, resulting in a higher value for zeta potential (Kaya and Yukselen., 2015). The points of zero charge of goethite and kaolinite were measured at pH 5.8 and 2.9, respectively, which agreed well with the values reported in the literature (Chng and Pumera, 2013; Liu et al., 2018b). GO remained negatively charged over the pH ranged from 2 to 10, because of the epoxy in the basal plane and hydroxyl groups in the carboxylic acid groups at the edge (Yang et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2016). Zeta potential values of GO decreased significantly from pH 2 to 6, while it remained very similar from pH 6 to 10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite

ce:section.ce:para:
Adsorption kinetic was used to examine adsorption rate and to examine the controlling mechanism of adsorption process (Sparks et al., 1980; Strawn et al., 1998). Thus, it is necessary to study the adsorption kinetic. The experimental data from the kinetic adsorption batch experiments were fitted with pseudo-first-order (Eq. (4)) (Fig. 2 A), pseudo-second-order (Eq. (5)) (Fig. 2B) and intra-particle diffusion model (Eq. (6)) (Fig. 2C) (Idris et al., 2013; Li et al., 2009; Hu et al., 2017).
(4)
log
(
q
e
−
q
t
)
= log
q
e
−
k
1
t
(5)
t
q
t
=
1
k
2
q
e
2
+
t
q
e
(6)
q
t
=
k
i
t
1
2
+ C

Where q
t (mg/g) was the adsorption amount of GO onto kaolinite and goethite at time t. q
e (mg/g) was the adsorption amount of GO at equilibrium time; k1 (min−1) was the pseudo-first-order constant, k2 (g mg−1 min−1) was pseudo-second-order kinetic rate constant, and ki (mg g−1 min−1/2) was the intraparticle diffusion rate constant. C was proportional to the boundary layer thickness.

Results were shown in Table S1. The calculated correlation coefficients (R2) presented that pseudo-second-order fitted better than pseudo-first-order for goethite and kaolinite, indicating that adsorption of GO onto goethite and kaolinite were governed by physicochemical interactions. The equilibrium time for goethite and kaolinite are within 75 and 720 min, respectively. The difference of equilibrium time may own to the stronger chemisorption between GO and goethite than that in GO and kaolinite. The equilibrium adsorption capacity for goethite (3.73 mg/g) was larger than that kaolinite (1.07 mg/g) at pH 5.0. This result indicated the interaction force between GO and goethite was larger than of GO and kaolinite. As shown in Fig. 1B, the zeta potential value was positive for goethite (∼+20 mV) and negative for kaolinite (∼−20 mV) and GO (∼−50 mV). Therefore, the electrostatic interaction between GO and goethite was attractive force while repulsive force between GO and kaolinite. The adsorption for GO on kaolinite may be contributed to chemisorption (Sotirelis and Chrysikopoulos, 2017).

The intra-particle diffusion model can further illustrate the rate-limiting step of the adsorption process (Duan et al., 2016). Fig. 2C showed two linear consecutive steps. The first part, the surface diffusion section, for goethite with a higher slope (0.19 mg g−1 min−1/2) than for kaolinite (0.094 mg g−1 min−1/2), indicating that the surface diffusion rate for GO toward goethite was faster than kaolinite (Liu et al., 2018a). This may be caused by attractive force between goethite and GO. The complexation of adsorption reaction dominated that the second part was a slow process (Duan et al., 2017). This result also confirmed chemisorption in the interaction process.

Fig. 2D showed the influence of pH on GO adsorption onto goethite and kaolinite. The decrease of GO mass adsorbed onto the minerals with increasing pH values was observed. At pH < 3.0, adsorption percentage (%) was above 90% for both kaolinite and goethite. Homoaggregation of GO and heteroaggregation between GO and minerals may own to the high adsorption capacity at this pH range. According to Wu's work (Wu et al., 2013), the addition of H+ may lead to homoaggregation of the GO sheets because of the protonation of -COOH at the edge. Besides, at pH < 3, surface charge for kaolinite and goethite were positive, therefore, electrostatic attraction dominated the interaction process (Konkena and Vasudevan, 2012). From pH 3 to 9, the adsorption percentage decreased as pH increased which was contributed to a decrease in the absolute zeta potential values by increasing the pH, as result of the reduction in the electrostatic interaction forces. The decreased adsorption capacity was caused by interaction between GO and minerals rather than homoaggreagtion because GO was stable at pH > 3 (Gao et al., 2018). To be noted that zeta potentials of kaolinite at pH 3 to 9 and goethite at pH 6 to 9 were negative, so the electrostatic interaction forces were electrostatic repulsive forces. Therefore, adsorption of GO on negative kaolinite and goethite particles from aqueous solutions result from chemisorptions. The abundant oxygen-containing groups in GO and the hydroxyl groups on the minerals surface can form strong hydrogen-bonding interactions, as well as Lewis acid base interactions (Yang et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2017b). The hydroxyl groups can be viewed as a Lewis base because of the lone electron pairs on the oxygen atoms, whereas GO layers can be considered as Lewis acid centers because of the withdrawal of electron density by the more electronegative oxygen groups (Yang et al., 2013; Hartono et al., 2009). Both hydrogen-bonding and Lewis acid base interactions overcome the charge repulsion and enable GO adsorption. In addition, adsorption percentage for goethite was higher than that for kaolinite at this range, suggesting the interaction force was larger between GO and goethite than kaolinite. After pH > 10, little GO can attach onto goethite and kaolinite, electrostatic repulsive forces may greater than hydrogen-bonding interaction and Lewis acid base interactions above pH 10.

Adsorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite at pH 5.0 and 7.0 under various concentrations of NaCl were shown on Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3A, adsorption (%) of GO on goethite increased by Na+ increase and reached equilibrium at 6 mM NaCl both at pH 5.0 and 7.0. The trend of GO adsorption on kaolinite (Fig. 3B) was similar with it on goethite, but the variation range of adsorption percentage was smaller for kaolinite than goethite. It is displayed that there are about 50% increase for goethite from 0 to 6 mM NaCl while just 20% increase for kaolinite. The absolute zeta potential value of GO decreased with increasing ionic strength, due to suppression of the electric double layer (Qi et al., 2014). And zeta potentials for kaolinite or goethite changed a little at 0–10 mM NaCl (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the double layer repulsion between GO and minerals reduced. Additionally, it has been reported that increasing ionic strength had little effect on the primary energy barriers but deepened the secondary-minimum wells between GO and minerals surface (Liu et al., 2013). As shown from Fig. 3D, the hydrodynamic diameters of GO and minerals changed a little from 0 to 10 mM NaCl, indicating aggregations of GO particles through primary minimum interactions were negligible. Therefore, GO particles could attach on the mineral surfaces through the secondary minimum attachment at high ionic strength (Liu et al., 2013).

Temperature is an important factor governing the adsorption process. The effect of temperature on the adsorption is shown in Fig. 3E and Fig. S3. Result showed that the increasing temperature caused an increase in adsorbed GO at the same minerals concentration, indicating that the process was endothermic. One of the reasons for the increase of adsorption (%) by temperature was increase the rate of diffusion of GO molecules across solution to the minerals particles surface (Doğan et al., 2004). In addition, a possible mechanism of interaction is the chemisorptions between GO and minerals could be favored at higher temperatures.

Adsorption isotherms of GO on goethite and kaolinite at pH 5.0 and 7.0 at 293 K are shown in Fig. 3F. Langmuir (
q
e
= b
q
max
C
e
/
( 1 + b
C
e
)
and Freundlich models (
q
e
=
K
F
C
e
n
) were applied to simulate the adsorption isotherms (Table S2) (Yu et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2016). From the relation coefficients (R2), the Langmuir model fits the experimental data better than the Freundlich model. This phenomenon suggests that monolayer adsorption occurred. The Freundlich model result (n < 1) suggested that the adsorption of GO onto minerals was a favorable process (Liu et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2016). Furthermore, a striking observation was that q max at pH 5.0 was larger than that at pH 7.0, which was also observed in adsorption kinetic experiment. It is attributed to the decreasing absolute zeta potential values with decreasing pH. For goethite, the difference at pH 5.0 and 7.0 was larger than kaolinite, which may be due to the zeta potentials at pH 5.0 was positive while it became negative at pH 7.0. For kaolinite, zeta potentials both at pH 5.0 and 7.0 are negative.

The thermodynamic behaviors of GO adsorption onto kaolinite and goethite were investigated from the temperature dependent adsorption isotherms (Fig. S4). The Gibb's free energy (ΔG0) can be calculated from
Δ
G
0
= Δ
H
0
− T Δ
S
0
, the entropy (ΔS0) and enthalpy (ΔH0) changes have been calculated by
L n
K
d
0
=
Δ
S
0
R
−
Δ
H
0
R T
, where Kd 0 is calculated by plotting lnKd versus C
e and extrapolating C
e to zero (Tan et al., 2018a; Zhu et al., 2019). The thermodynamic parameters calculated from the equations at three temperatures are listed in Table S3. The positive ΔH0 means that the processes of adsorption GO on kaolinite and goethite are endothermic, confirming by that a higher temperature promotes the adsorption process. The negative ΔG0 indicated that the adsorption process was spontaneous and became more negative with increasing temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Desorption of GO on goethite and kaolinite

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 showed the desorption kinetics of GO on kaolinite and goethite. Result showed that desorption was characterized by a rapid reaction (short-term) followed by a slower reaction (long-term), which was similar to the adsorption reaction (Fig. 4A). The observed rapid desorption of GO from kaolinite may be due to weakly bound and the slower reaction was contributed by surface complexes. Desorption percentage for kaolinite was higher than that for goethite which indicated complexation between GO and goethite was stronger than it between GO and kaolinite.

The first-order rate equation was used to model the data as two first-order reactions (Sparks et al., 1980):
(7)
Ln
( M
e
t
/ M
e
o
)
= −
k
d
( t )
where Met (mg/g) was the amount of GO sorbed at desorption time t (min), Me0 (mg/g) was the amount of GO sorbed at time 0, and kd (min−1) was the apparent desorption coefficient. Fig. 4B showed the result for both the rapid and slow desorption processes, and desorption rate coefficients fitted the first-order well. k2 for GO desorption was less than k1, which may be attributed to the desorption of weakly bound.

The amounts of GO desorbed from the goethite and kaolinite were greatly affected by pH (2–9) (Fig. 4C). The goethite and kaolinite behaved similarly in GO desorption in relation to pH change, although the GO desorption ability changed more or less with pH. From pH 2 to 4, a little GO can desorb from both goethite and kaolinite and then desorption percentage increased by pH increase from 4 to 9. To be noted that, for kaolinite the desorption percentage achieved equilibrium but not 100% from pH 8. The interaction force at pH 2 to 4 may strongest own to the low desorption percentage and interaction decreased by pH increase from 4 to 9. The equilibrium desorption percentage did not achieve 100%, suggesting part of GO cannot desorb from minerals. The higher desorption percentage for kaolinite than goethite indicated weaker interaction force between GO and kaolinite.

The effect of ionic strength on GO desorption from goethite and kaolinite at pH 5.0 and 7.0 were investigated at 0–10 mM NaCl and results were shown in Fig. 4D and E. Result showed that desorption (%) on goethite decreased from 0 to 2 mM Na+ and then almost kept constant at pH 5.0 or 7.0. For kaolinite, the desorption (%) decreased obviously from 0 to 2 mM Na+ and then became a slow decrease. After adding Na+, part of GO in suspension which did not adsorbed by minerals previously would be adsorbed. Because with increasing ionic strength of the suspension, the double layer was compressed, resulting in smaller distances between GO and minerals. According to this, the desorption (%) decreased from 0 to 2 mM Na+. Above 2 mM NaCl, desorption (%) for kaolinite decrease while for goethite changed little indicated that the interaction force between GO and goethite was larger than that between kaolinite.

Fig. 4F showed the temperature effect on GO desorption (%) from goethite and kaolinite. At the same temperatures, desorption (%) for kaolinite was also higher than goethite, which indicated GO can be easily desorbed from kaolinite than goethite. The harder desorption by higher temperature indicated that the chemical bond hardly broke at the high temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for interaction between GO and minerals

ce:section.ce:para: Desorption experiments were conducted for a better understanding of the interaction between minerals and GO. Hysteresis index (HI) was calculated by the equitation (HI = ndesorp/nadsorb) (Hamidpour et al., 2010). The lower index values, the more difficult to desorb GO from the minerals. The desorption-adsorption isotherms for goethite and kaolinite at pH 5.0 and 293 K were showed in Fig. S5. HI for goethite (0.77) was lower than kaolinte (0.98), which suggested that GO was more difficult to desorb from goethite than from kaolinite (Hamidpour et al., 2010).

o investigate the interaction mechanism between GO and minerals, SEM and FTIR (Fig. 5 ) were used to examine the morphology and chemical structure changes. As it can be seen from SEM images, the complexes were observed when GO and minerals were coexisting. Fig. 5A and B showed the images for complex between GO and kaolinite/goethite. Fig. 5A displayed kaolinite was heteroaggreagted with GO and wrapped by GO because the lateral size of GO was much larger than that of kaolinite. Fig. 5B showed the heteroaggreagtion between GO and goethite was more obvious and similarly with kaolinite, goethite was also wrapped by GO. Fig. S6 was TEM and element mapping pattern of the GO-goethite aggregates and GO-kaolinite aggregates. Result showed that GO-goethite aggregates mainly consisted O, Fe, C elements and GO-goethite aggregates mainly consisted Al, Si, O, C. Furthermore, the contents of C elements were larger on GO-goethite aggregates than that on GO-goethite aggregates.

Fig. 5C and D showed the FTIR of GO, goethite, kaolinite, and after adsorption and desorption of GO. After GO adsorption, a new peak at 1443 cm−1 was appeared at GO-goethite complex which was caused by –COOH in GO binding with Fe element in goethite (Kang and Xing, 2008). Besides, there were two small peaks at 2921 and 2853 cm−1 in complex after GO adsorption and desorption. Furthermore, a new peak at 1750 cm−1 was appeared in GO-goethite and GO-kaolinite complex. These results suggested that GO was successfully adsorbed on goethite and kaolinite. To be pointed that, the peak at 1750 cm−1 disappeared after desorption while two small peaks at 2921 and 2853 cm−1 (Fig. S7) are still observed. Therefore, this adsorption is a partial irreversible process.

The results of this study demonstrated that part of interaction between GO and minerals was irreversible. The equilibrium adsorption capacity for goethite (3.73 mg/g) was larger than that kaolinite (1.07 mg/g) at pH 5.0, indicating the interaction force between GO and goethite was larger than it between GO and kaolinite. Adsorption of GO on negative kaolinite and goethite particles at pH > pHzp from aqueous solutions result from chemisorptions. Ionic strength and temperature have obvious influence on adsorption and desorption. Desorption hysteresis was observed, which could be explained by the formation of GO−minerals complex. This reslt provides useful insights into the environment behavior of GO and its fate and health risk in the environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21677146, 21876178), and Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2019.03.104.
